mainText:
**Ossining, New York, in the ‘Unwoke’ 1950s**
*By Thomas Dichter / March 28, 2026*

The only time I and my fellow trombone players were noticed was during a parade. Because the trombone’s slide extends a good three feet beyond the marcher playing it, we marched in the front row to keep from hitting others in the band with our slides. John Philip Sousa’s marches—especially “Washington Post,” “The Thunderer,” and “The Stars and Stripes Forever”—had great trombone parts, and that is what we played every Memorial Day, when the members of the Ossining High School Band put on our heavy uniforms and our white buck shoes and marched up and down Main Street in the well-attended parade.

It was the late 1950s, a time of peace and unprecedented prosperity. We may have been the first teenage generation in the world with our own cars, living in a world without serious problems—or so we thought; we had not yet heard of Vietnam. Aside from a general fear of communism, politics were relatively benign; while not all our parents liked Ike enough to vote for him, we were mostly fine with our president, and trusted him. We had enough money from various jobs, which were easy to get, to go to the movies, put gas in our cars, hold cigarettes, and date. My high school class of 158 consisted mostly of half Protestants, with 16 percent Italian American Catholics, 15 percent Irish American Catholics, 9 percent Jewish, and 8 percent African American. Although we understood those distinctions, they seemed to play little role in our daily experiences. There were mean kids and a few bullies, but by and large, we got along. We did not talk about or even acknowledge the fact that the black students lived in just one part of town, down the hill toward the railroad tracks, nor did we speak about the rich and poor folks who had their own areas. The identities that mattered more were whether you were a jock or a brain, whether you were cute or pretty, and whether you could dance. What animated us were sports, music, parties (and the chance to "make out"), and cars. Some boys could take apart an engine and put it back together over a weekend.

At the same time, we liked going to school and enjoyed the school itself. Ossining High School’s Ivy League Gothic style influenced us; it was built in 1930, designed by James Gamble Rogers, who also designed Yale's Harkness Tower. "Blackboard Jungle" was not our world. There were no guns or knives, and the closest we got to drugs was an illicit six-pack. While we did not view education as fun, we understood the purpose—to learn history and read books, along with basic skills such as typing, diagramming sentences, and calculating the area of a triangle. There were shop classes for the boys and home economics for the girls, where we learned how to measure wood or flour. Many of us felt teachers were part of a decent and perhaps noble profession. We even had a club called Future Teachers of America, and many in our class became teachers. It was somewhat assumed that some of us would go to college, while others would not. But that distinction didn't carry a heavy weight—those of us who went to college didn’t believe we were better than those who didn't.

What struck me was how much we collectively believed in our American-ness without question. We seemed to know, even if vaguely, that we could relate to people from other parts of the U.S. Somehow, despite Ossining's geographical closeness to New York City, we felt similar to towns in Idaho, Missouri, Maine, or Nebraska. Perhaps our sense of American-ness was strengthened by the typicality of Ossining in the 1950s, with one notable difference: Sing Sing Prison, opened in 1826, situated right on the Hudson River. In fact, the town was named Sing Sing until 1901, when, perhaps slightly embarrassed by its association with the prison, it changed its name to Ossining.

Our main street reflected the self-contained nature of towns at the time, featuring an imposing bank where Main met Albany Post Road, clothing stores, a five-and-dime, a movie theater, and Kipp’s Pharmacy with its classic soda fountain. We were likely influenced by the national TV programs available at the time, our movies, teachers, and textbooks that often skipped over the darker parts of America’s past. Were we conforming to our peers’ rosy collated groupthink? Our varsity football team had four black me
